perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will continue in full force and effect until both parties agree in writing to terminate this agreement and for one -year thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will continue in full force and effect until both parties agree in writing to terminate this agreement and for ten years thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality term to ten years after termination may be unreasonably long and thus conflict with trade secret laws or general principles of contract law, especially in Utah, where the agreement is governed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-1 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah's trade secret law defines trade secrets, and while a confidentiality agreement can protect them, a blanket ten-year term may be challenged if the information no longer qualifies as a trade secret (e.g., becomes generally known) within that period. The modification introduces an unreasonable restriction, potentially conflicting with the statute's intent to protect genuinely valuable trade secrets, not outdated information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-2 (2022) - Definitions. :: 2022 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code 2022 Utah Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 2 - Definitions. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the confidentiality term to ten years, which may be an unreasonably long time and contradict Utah's trade secret law. The Utah Code § 13-24-2 defines 'trade secret'. The Law Explanation suggests that a blanket ten-year term may be challenged if the information no longer qualifies as a trade secret within that period, because it becomes generally known. Therefore, the extended term could be seen as an unreasonable restriction, potentially conflicting with the statute's intent to protect genuinely valuable trade secrets, not outdated information. The changed text introduces an inconsistency regarding the protection of trade secrets beyond a reasonable timeframe, thus contradicting the spirit of trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: If a Party or any third party to whom such Party has provided Confidential Information becomes legally compelled (by oral question, deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process or by rule, regulation or other applicable law) to disclose any Confidential Information, such Party shall promptly notify the other Party of such requirement before any disclosure is made so that the other Party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy or may waive compliance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: If a Party or any third party to whom such Party has provided Confidential Information becomes legally compelled (by oral question, deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process or by rule, regulation or other applicable law) to disclose any Confidential Information, such Party shall notify the other Party of such requirement within 3 days after disclosure is made.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification timeline to 'within 3 days after disclosure' directly conflicts with the duty to provide prompt notice *before* disclosure, hindering the other party's opportunity to seek protective measures. This conflicts with legal standards for responding to subpoenas and court orders.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 45(d)(2)(B) requires a party withholding subpoenaed information based on privilege or protection to 'expressly assert' the claim and describe the nature of the documents, communications, or tangible things not produced in a manner that, without revealing information itself privileged or protected, will enable other parties to assess the claim. Disclosing the information first and notifying after is not adequate to meet the party's obligation to enable assessment of the privilege claim.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original contract's intent to allow the other party to seek protective measures *before* confidential information is disclosed under legal compulsion. By changing the notification timeline to 'within 3 days after disclosure,' the modified text removes the opportunity for the other party to prevent the disclosure in the first place. This violates the standard of providing an opportunity to respond to legal processes like subpoenas effectively. While the scraped snippets don't explicitly mention the timing of notification in relation to protective orders, the general emphasis on proper subpoena procedure implies that parties should have an opportunity to respond appropriately before information is disclosed. The Perturbation Explanation strongly highlights this conflict, which makes the contradiction impactful.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will continue in full force and effect until both parties agree in writing to terminate this agreement and for one -year thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will continue in full force and effect until both parties agree in writing to terminate this agreement and for one month thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one month may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially violating the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-2(2)(b)(ii)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines misappropriation as disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent by a person who knew or had reason to know that their knowledge of the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. While the agreement establishes a duty of confidentiality, a one-month post-termination period may be deemed unreasonable for protecting certain types of trade secrets, effectively undermining the protection afforded by the Act if challenged.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period to one month, which, as explained, could be deemed unreasonable under the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The Law Explanation argues that this undermines the protection afforded by the Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'Misappropriation' to include the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent by a person who knew or had reason to know their knowledge was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy. The one-month period, by being potentially inadequate, weakens the "duty to maintain secrecy," creating a significant contradiction and potential violation of the Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any third party to whom such Party has provided Confidential Information, or any portion thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any third party to whom such Party has provided Confidential Information, or any portion thereof, regardless of whether the breach was foreseeable or preventable.
perturbation.explanation: Holding a party strictly liable for *any* breach by a third party, even if unforeseeable or unpreventable, may be unenforceable under general contract law principles of foreseeability and causation, especially in Utah. This is because Utah contract law generally requires some degree of fault or control for liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Restatement is not law, Utah courts often look to the Restatement for guidance in contract law. Section 351 limits damages to those that are foreseeable. Holding a party liable for any breach whatsoever removes that reasonable limitation.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text holds a party responsible for breaches by third parties to whom they provided confidential information. The changed text expands this responsibility to include breaches that are unforeseeable or unpreventable, potentially creating a strict liability standard. The Law Explanation states that Utah courts often look to the Restatement, and Section 351 limits damages to those that are foreseeable. By removing the foreseeability limitation, the changed text contradicts general contract law principles, potentially making the party liable for events outside their control, which conflicts with Utah contract law principles.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby acknowledge and agree that, in the event of any breach of this Agreement by either Party, including, without limitation, the actual or threatened disclosure of a Party's Confidential Information without the express prior written consent of that Paity, such Party will suffer irreparable harm and injury and no remedy at law will afford it adequate protection against, or appropriate compensation for, such injury.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereby acknowledge and agree that, in the event of any breach of this Agreement by either Party, including, without limitation, the actual or threatened disclosure of a Party's Confidential Information without the express prior written consent of that Paity, monetary damages shall be the sole and exclusive remedy.
perturbation.explanation: Stipulating monetary damages as the sole remedy removes the possibility of injunctive relief, which is often crucial for protecting confidential information. This limitation may be unenforceable if monetary damages are inadequate, particularly when dealing with trade secrets where the harm is ongoing and difficult to quantify. Utah law allows for injunctive relief in trade secret misappropriation cases.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Utah Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-3
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-3 explicitly allows for injunctive relief to prevent or restrain actual or threatened misappropriation of a trade secret. The modified clause, by limiting the remedy to monetary damages, directly contradicts the statute's provision for injunctive relief.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-3 (2022) - Injunctive relief. :: 2022 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code 2022 Utah Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 3 - Injunctive relief. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-3 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-3. Injunctive relief. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. perturbation.justification: The changed text limits remedies to monetary damages, directly contradicting Utah Code § 13-24-3 (2022), which explicitly allows for injunctive relief in cases of actual or threatened misappropriation. The original text acknowledges the potential for irreparable harm and the inadequacy of monetary damages, implicitly allowing for injunctive relief. By stipulating that monetary damages are the sole remedy, the changed text eliminates the possibility of injunctive relief, thereby contradicting the legal provision in Utah law that grants courts the power to issue injunctions to prevent trade secret misappropriation, as stated in the provided snippet: 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined.'
